notes: DEC's flagship 32-bit computer architecture; foundation for all enterprise strategy; VAX-11/780 was reference benchmark (1 VUP); scalable from MicroVAX to VAX 8700
DEC's primary OS; Olsen positioned as more modern than Unix (12 vs 19 years old); claimed fully transportable across VAX range. Now OpenVMS, maintained by VSI.
DEC's proprietary networking protocol family; being evolved toward OSI standards; presented as fundamentally easier to manage than IBM SNA

DEC's windowed GUI environment based on X Windows standard; company-wide program for VMS and ULTRIX; 50+ internal projects implementing it at time of document. Failed to gain market traction vs Microsoft Windows.
DEC's OLTP product strategy and system suite; major announcement at this meeting; Peter Kastner led marketing work. Built on VAXcluster + ACMS + Rdb.
DEC's transaction processing monitor/server; key component of DECtp architecture; queued and managed transaction requests
DEC's clustered computing system; 6600 installed worldwide at time of document; 16% of all VAX nodes in clusters; 64% of top 200 accounts had VAXcluster; key TP high-availability platform
DEC's integrated office automation suite; key component of distributed application environment; being ported to DECwindows
DEC's Unix variant; positioned as equal partner to VMS; Olsen said DEC sold both VMS and Unix with equal enthusiasm; primarily on workstations
DEC's architectural framework for unified application environment; provided common software standards across VMS and ULTRIX; foundation for NAS
DEC's strategy for integrating non-DEC architectures (MS-DOS, SNA, Cray, Apple) into enterprise-wide computing; three classes: apps access, business comms, info/resource sharing
DEC's technical workstation line; 90% ran VMS at time; DEC claimed fastest-growing workstation vendor surpassing Sun; leaped over Apollo to #2 position
DEC's small-form VAX systems; used as front-end processors in distributed TP architecture
DEC's MS-DOS compatible PC; offered for users already needing MS-DOS applications; niche product in DEC's desktop lineup
DEC's character/graphics terminals; shipping 500000+ per year at time of document; all exceeded forecasts; presented as long-term market despite PC competition
DEC's high-speed interconnect for VAXcluster systems; enabled shared storage and redundancy
DEC's relational database; key component of TP strategy; industry trend toward RDBMS for TP noted (replacing flat files). Oracle acquired it in 1994.
DEC's architectural framework for distributed TP; front-end (forms/stimulus), transaction server (monitor), resource manager (database); extensible to hundreds of TPS
DEC's internal global network; 445 locations, 32 countries, 28000 computers, 80000 users; presented as proof-of-concept for enterprise-wide computing vision
Windowing standard from MIT Project Athena; adopted by DECwindows as foundation; managed by X Windows Consortium (DEC + 23 vendors). DECwindows built on X11.
Olsen described as 19 years old, originally for PDP-7; DEC committed to selling VMS and ULTRIX (Unix) with equal enthusiasm; customer demand driven by vendor-independence desire
IBM's proprietary network architecture; characterized by DEC as 'closed, complex, IBM-controlled, hierarchical' and not meeting real enterprise networking needs
International networking standards; DEC evolving DECnet toward OSI; positioned as open alternative to IBM SNA
Named as NAS integration target; DEC offered VMS Services for MS-DOS as 'leading integration product in the market, won numerous awards'
IBM/Microsoft new OS; referenced as costing $8000 to upgrade a PC-AT; DEC committed to supporting OS/2 once organized; ultimately failed vs Windows
Named NAS integration target; DEC announced joint development with Apple; cited as 'increasingly attractive for personal computing with modern UI and ease of use'
Core market concept; $26B market at time, expected to reach $60B by 1991; metrics: transactions-per-second, dollars-per-TPS, response time; 90-95% of transactions must complete in 1-2 seconds
DEC's PC integration product; claimed to be 'leading integration product in the market' with numerous awards
